                                                                                                                     (INGLÉS)

                                              CURSO 2023-2024

     Instrucciones:        a) Duración: 1 hora y 30 minutos.
                           b) Todas las cuestiones deben responderse en el papel entregado para la realización del examen y
                           nunca en los folios que contienen los enunciados.
                           c) Este examen consta de varios bloques. Debe responder a las preguntas que se indican en cada uno.
                           d) La puntuación está indicada en cada uno de los apartados.
                           e) No se permite el uso de diccionario.


                                                El examen consta de 3 Bloques (A, B y C)
En cada bloque (Comprehension, Use of English y Writing) se plantean varias preguntas, de las que se deberá responder al número que se
indica en cada uno. En caso de responder más cuestiones de las requeridas, serán tenidas en cuenta las respondidas en primer lugar hasta
alcanzar dicho número. Las preguntas han de ser respondidas en su totalidad: si la pregunta tiene dos secciones, hay que responder ambas.
BLOQUE A (Comprensión lectora)
Puntuación máxima: 4 puntos
Debe responderse a las 8 preguntas de uno de los 2 textos propuestos.
COMPREHENSION (4 points). CHOOSE TEXT 1 OR TEXT 2 AND ANSWER ALL THE QUESTIONS FROM THAT TEXT ONLY.
TEXT 1: SAVING ABU SIMBEL

 1           In the early 1960s, Egypt had to improve agriculture and provide electricity for its increasing population. In order to do so, the
 2      country planned to build a huge dam across the Nile River that would flood an area with important archaeological sites. In the eyes
 3      of the Egyptian government, the destruction of the treasures, while regrettable, was necessary. Christiane Desroches-Noblecourt,
 4      the female chief curator of Egyptian antiquities at the Louvre Museum, appealed to Egyptian officials not to resign themselves to
 5      this catastrophic loss of their cultural heritage.
 6           Christiane proposed to them the most difficult archaeological rescue in history —a project of almost unthinkable magnitude and
 7      complexity consisting in moving the fragile sandstone temple of Abu Simbel to higher ground. On top of the enormous engineering
 8      problems, the project required cooperation among countries at a time of escalating global political tensions. But that didn’t stop her.
 9           After months of countless meetings, Christiane eventually won the support of UNESCO and Egypt’s Minister of Culture, who
10      persuaded the Egyptian president to approve the rescue plan. This was a major step, but the project was doomed to fail without
11      massive financial help from Western countries, particularly the United States. It was then that an unexpected saviour, Jacqueline
12      Kennedy, appeared on the scene. Thanks to her influence, her husband, President John F. Kennedy, called on US Congress to
13      authorise money to ensure the rescue.
14           Ironically, the two women who played crucial roles in this landmark rescue apparently had no idea of each other’s moves. Both
15      Christiane and Jacqueline had worked behind the scenes. Neither of them ever sought or received public attention for their
16      achievement, caring less about the credit than getting the job done.

CHOOSE AND WRITE THE CORRECT OPTION (A, B, C or D). (0.5 points each)
1. According to the text, Abu Simbel…
    (a) was buried under the sand.                      (b) was damaged during the rescue.
    (c) went through a complex relocation.              (d) was moved abroad.
2. According to the text, Jacqueline Kennedy…
    (a) negotiated with UNESCO.                         (b) convinced Christiane to save the temple.
    (c) tried to acquire the temple.                    (d) helped get the project financed.

ARE THESE STATEMENTS TRUE OR FALSE? JUSTIFY YOUR ANSWERS WITH THE PRECISE WORDS OR PHRASES FROM THE
TEXT, OR USE YOUR OWN WORDS. (0.5 points each)
3. The initial dam project involved leaving ancient treasures under the water.
4. International conflicts added to the difficulty of the mission.
5. Egypt had enough resources to carry out the rescue operation.
6. Christiane and Jacqueline worked side by side.

7. FIND IN THE TEXT: (0.5 points)
    7.1. ONE SYNONYM FOR: “lamentable” (adjective)
    7.2. ONE OPPOSITE FOR: “gain” (noun)
8. FIND IN THE TEXT: (0.5 points)
    8.1. ONE WORD MEANING: “to make certain, guarantee.”
    8.2. ONE WORD MEANING: “a person who rescues another person or a thing from danger or harm.”

TEXT 2: THE STRENGTH OF WEAK SOCIAL TIES

 1          I’ve been working as a psychotherapist since 1988. Through the years, I’ve dealt with hundreds of patients who come to my
 2     practice because they feel lonely. Many report finding relief in brief, casual encounters. That’s the case of Alice, who lived alone
 3     for years after a divorce. Her family lived away from her, and she described feeling terribly lonesome, especially during the holidays.
 4     One day, I asked her what helped cheer her up. “I love going to the library and chatting with Maria, the librarian, about historical
 5     novels or cozy mysteries. I always leave feeling a warm connection with her.”
 6          Another patient, Sherry, gets great joy from her Sunday breakfasts at a local diner: “The manager’s so nice. She calls me
 7     ‘Sweety’ and brings me extra whipped cream for my pancakes. I feel uplifted.”
 8          As for me, when I shop for groceries, I always go to a cashier I’ve chatted with for years. Our shared kindness and familiarity
 9     give me a sense of community. What’s more, even those we meet only once can leave a lasting impression. I’ll never forget
10     discussing Don Quixote on an airplane with a well-read economics professor.
11          Relationships like these, generally referred to as “weak social ties”, have been proved to be just as important to life satisfaction
12     as core or strong ties. When we feel blue or lonely, we tend to turn down social engagements, usually to avoid the imagined
13     embarrassment of being the only sad person in a group. But saying yes, despite the hesitation, offers an opportunity to feel less
14     isolated. Staying mindful of our strong and weak ties allows us to benefit from the comfort and the sense of community they provide.

CHOOSE AND WRITE THE CORRECT OPTION (A, B, C or D). (0.5 points each)
9. Alice…
    (a) has been a client of the author’s since 1988.   (b) feels all alone when her family goes away on vacation.
    (c) and her therapist go to the same library.       (d) enjoys casual conversation about shared interests.
10. The therapist…
    (a) sticks to a routine when paying at the store.   (b) feels lonely herself in her daily life.
    (c) read a whole book during a flight.              (d) recommends turning weak ties into strong ones.

ARE THESE STATEMENTS TRUE OR FALSE? JUSTIFY YOUR ANSWERS WITH THE PRECISE WORDS OR PHRASES FROM THE
TEXT, OR USE YOUR OWN WORDS. (0.5 points each)
11. Sherry receives special attention at her usual restaurant.
12. Casual encounters with strangers may be memorable.
13. Short routine interactions are less rewarding than strong personal relationships.
14. People often refuse to mix with others if they are feeling low.

15. FIND IN THE TEXT: (0.5 points)
    15.1. ONE SYNONYM FOR: “awfully” (adverb)
    15.2. ONE OPPOSITE FOR: “determination” (noun)
16. FIND IN THE TEXT: (0.5 points)
    16.1. ONE WORD MEANING: “someone whose job is to receive or pay out money in a shop.”
    16.2. ONE WORD MEANING: “food and other goods sold in a shop or at a supermarket.”

17. FILL IN THE GAPS WITH THE CORRECT FORM OF THE VERB IN BRACKETS:
    17.1. “I would rather you … near me.” (not smoke)
    17.2. “I wish I … abroad last year.” (travel)

    18.1. “We suggested … for an hour.” gathering / to gather / to gathering / gather
    18.2. “I can’t remember the meaning of this word. I’ll look it … in the dictionary.” down / up / for / out

    for her class that day. She is usually on time.”
23. REWRITE THE FOLLOWING SENTENCE WITHOUT CHANGING ITS MEANING. BEGIN AS INDICATED: “They spent a week
    repainting the kitchen.” It took...
24. COMPLETE THE FOLLOWING SENTENCE: “We’ll have to postpone the event so that…”
25. THERE ARE TWO MISTAKES IN THE FOLLOWING SENTENCE. FIND THE MISTAKES AND REWRITE THE SENTENCE
    CORRECTLY: “When King Charles arrived to the British Museum, everyone were waiting at the door.”
26. TURN THE FOLLOWING SENTENCE INTO DIRECT SPEECH: “Grace apologized to Will for having forgotten his birthday.”
27. TURN THE FOLLOWING SENTENCE INTO REPORTED SPEECH: “‘Your exam will take place next week, Peter,’ the teacher
    said.”
28. USE THE WORDS IN THE BOXES TO MAKE A MEANINGFUL SENTENCE. USE ALL AND ONLY THE WORDS IN THE BOXES
    WITHOUT CHANGING THEIR FORM:

                           got     dress     my     Armani    wedding     designed    sister     by      her




BLOQUE C (Redacción)
Puntuación máxima: 3 puntos
Deberá realizar una redacción de un mínimo de 120 palabras de UNO de los dos temas propuestos SOLAMENTE.

WRITING (3 points)
29. WRITE A COMPOSITION OF AT LEAST 120 WORDS ABOUT ONE OF THE TOPICS PROPOSED AND FOCUS ON IT. CHOOSE
ONE TOPIC ONLY:
    29.1. Which places in the world do you find fascinating? Give reasons.
    29.2. What makes a good friend for you? Explain.

TEXT 1: DAYLIGHT SAVING TIME

 1          Spring marks many changes and, for many people around the world, the beginning of Daylight Saving Time (DST). The idea
 2      behind the clock shift is to make better use of sunlight.
 3          Benjamin Franklin is often credited as the first to suggest the change in 1784. When he was an American delegate in Paris,
 4      he felt he was wasting his Parisian mornings by staying in bed after the sun had come up. He ironically proposed that the French
 5      should fire cannons at sunrise to wake up people, so that they would go to bed earlier and use fewer candles at night.
 6          It wasn't until resources became limited during World War I that Germany decided to go ahead with a clock-changing plan,
 7      implementing the first DST in 1916. Most of Europe followed their example in a matter of weeks, and the United States did the
 8      same in 1918.
 9          Nowadays, DST still has fervent supporters who claim that it helps drive the economy. Conversely, it’s often argued that, apart
10      from the legion of missed meetings and sleepy citizens, DST causes adverse effects on our health and safety. Studies reveal that
11      changing clocks brings about an increase in heart attacks and fatal car accidents. DST opponents even question whether there
12      have ever been energy savings at all.
13          All in all, the time shift is becoming more and more unpopular around the world, since people today are fond of going out after
14      work and it’s obviously nicer doing so when it’s lighter outside. Recently, a survey showed 80% of Europeans were against DST,
15      and every October the matter is a controversial topic, with people either expressing enthusiasm about sunny mornings or
16      complaining about shorter days.

    (a) seems to benefit our health.                  (b) was proposed by the French government.
    (c) helps us sleep better.                        (d) has been argued to improve the economy.
2. According to the text, Benjamin Franklin…
    (a) suggested changing sleeping habits.           (b) was against changing clocks.
    (c) loved to sleep in the morning.                (d) made the French alter their customs.

ARE THESE STATEMENTS TRUE OR FALSE? JUSTIFY YOUR ANSWERS WITH THE PRECISE WORDS OR PHRASES FROM THE
TEXT, OR USE YOUR OWN WORDS. (0.5 points each)
3. According to the text, clocks change in the spring to make the most of natural light.
4. The Germans put the clock shift into effect to help fight the shortage of supplies.
5. People may even die due to Daylight Saving Time (DST).
6. According to the text, Daylight Saving Time (DST) fits nicely in our current lifestyle.

    7.2. ONE SYNONYM FOR: “crash” (noun)

TEXT 1: THE ETHICS OF TRUE CRIME

 1            True crime TV shows have been so popular in the past few years that they have become the object of academic study.
 2      Professor of Media Ethics Whitney Phillips claims that the reason for the current success of this genre, as compared to novels and
 3      movies, is the reality factor: audiences enjoy feeling engaged in an actual mystery happening before them.
 4            Research has found that white women are the largest demographic group consuming true crime. The explanation is that, since
 5      white women have anxiety about potential threats, they turn to true crime to feel better prepared in case anything violent were to
 6      happen to them. The fact that the target audience is basically white women determines the profile of typical victims in true crime
 7      —white and female. After all, audiences prefer to watch cases they can relate to. Phillips argues that this produces the “missing
 8      white woman syndrome,” that is, the idea that there’s only one type of victim whose story matters. The media reinforce this with
 9      many stories and updates about white female victims, while stories about marginalized groups like Indigenous women are rarely
10      shown, if at all. Even white women who are missing or murdered receive little media attention when they’re sex workers, because
11      they’re perceived as less valuable than “innocent” white women.

13      marketable content. As one victim said, “My trauma’s not your entertainment. Awareness is a whole different story.”

 1            If you made a reservation at a Michelin-starred restaurant, you might wonder why an elite restaurant guide has the same name

 3      intent of recommending the highest-quality restaurants. Actually, it was a clever publicity campaign now legendary for its success.
 4            The origin of the Michelin Star goes back to the early days of the automobile. French brothers André and Édouard Michelin
 5      founded their self-named tire company in 1889. It was a pioneering move considering that there were fewer than 3,000 automobiles
 6      in France at the time. Soon the brothers realized that if people drove more, the tires would eventually deteriorate, which would, as
 7      a result, increase Michelin’s sales. So, much like bloggers do today, they launched the Michelin Guide in 1900 to achieve that goal.
 8            The guide contained information that any motorist would needa detailed map, a compendium of mechanics and fuel stations,
 9      instructions on how to replace a flat tire, and suitable restaurants and hotels to eat and stay while journeying across France. There
10      was nothing quite like that in the market, so it became an instant hit.
11            In 1926, the Michelins introduced the concept of using a Michelin Star to rank restaurants. Most of this information came from
12      the company’s traveling salesmen, who spent much of their time on the roads and were, therefore, highly reliable sources. By the
13      late 1920s, Michelin’s restaurant recommendations had become so influential that the brothers started to use undercover
14      reviewers, now known as inspectors, to determine if a restaurant was a fine dining establishment. The ratings put in place in the
15      1930s stand to this dayone star means the restaurant is “worth a stop,” two means it’s “worth taking a side trip,” and three means
16      it’s “worth a special journey.”

18      consistent experience in terms of product quality, mastery of cooking techniques and the personality of the chef. What’s more, a

COMPREHENSION (4 points). READ THE TEXT AND ANSWER ALL THE QUESTIONS.
THE YEAR WITHOUT A SUMMER

 1           Snow in June, freezing temperatures in July, and a killer frost in August marked 1816 as the year without a summer for millions
 2      of people in North America and Europe. The unusual cold was later understood to be caused by the eruption of Mount Tambora
 3      in Indonesia in April 1815, the largest volcanic eruption in recorded history. When a volcano erupts, it releases sulfur dioxide, which
 4      can rise to the stratosphere. There, it reacts with water vapor to form sulfate aerosols that remain in the atmosphere, blocking
 5      sunlight and cooling the Earth’s surface.
 6           1816 was the coldest year in at least the last 250 years, according to a study. Each season showed temperature anomalies,
 7      but the summer brought the most drastic change, with the lowest summer temperature recorded in Europe between 1766 and
 8      2000. Cold, wet conditions in Central and Western Europe and even North America led to crop failure and the death of farm
 9      animals. It was called the “last great subsistence crisis in the Western World.” In addition to food shortages, the climate change
10      caused by the eruption resulted in disease, mass migration, and religious revivals as people tried to make sense of it all.
11           That year without a summer profoundly impacted the science and culture of the time. Agricultural failure was widespread, and
12      the cost of food jumped. This also increased the price of grain for horses, which were the main transportation source by then. This
13      might have helped inspire the invention of the bicycle by Karl Drais in 1817.
14           That exceptional summer also inspired something else: Gothic tales. The writers Lord Byron, Claire Clairmont, Dr. John
15      Polidori, Percy Shelley, and Mary Godwin (before she married Shelley) were staying in a villa overlooking Lake Geneva in
16      Switzerland. But the weather at their idyllic setting was depressing. During one of their discussions, Byron suggested that each
17      member of the restless group write a ghost story to share. Within a few weeks, Godwin had written Frankenstein, Byron composed
18      his poem “Darkness,” and Polidori wrote his short story The Vampyre.
19           The summer of 1816 teaches us that volcanic eruptions certainly affect climate change. With today’s global temperatures at
20      record highs, another massive eruption such as Mount Tambora’s would even likely pause human-made warming, though the
21      effects would eventually vanish.

 1           Snow in June, freezing temperatures in July, and a killer frost in August marked 1816 as the year without a summer for millions
 2      of people in North America and Europe. The unusual cold was later understood to be caused by the eruption of Mount Tambora
 3      in Indonesia in April 1815, the largest volcanic eruption in recorded history. When a volcano erupts, it releases sulfur dioxide, which
 4      can rise to the stratosphere. There, it reacts with water vapor to form sulfate aerosols that remain in the atmosphere, blocking
 5      sunlight and cooling the Earth’s surface.
 6           1816 was the coldest year in at least the last 250 years, according to a study. Each season showed temperature anomalies,
 7      but the summer brought the most drastic change, with the lowest summer temperature recorded in Europe between 1766 and
 8      2000. Cold, wet conditions in Central and Western Europe and even North America led to crop failure and the death of farm
 9      animals. It was called the “last great subsistence crisis in the Western World.” In addition to food shortages, the climate change
10      caused by the eruption resulted in disease, mass migration, and religious revivals as people tried to make sense of it all.
11           That year without a summer profoundly impacted the science and culture of the time. Agricultural failure was widespread, and
12      the cost of food jumped. This also increased the price of grain for horses, which were the main transportation source by then. This
13      might have helped inspire the invention of the bicycle by Karl Drais in 1817.

15      Polidori, Percy Shelley, and Mary Godwin (before she married Shelley) were staying in a villa overlooking Lake Geneva in

17      member of the restless group write a ghost story to share. Within a few weeks, Godwin had written Frankenstein, Byron composed

THE FORGOTTEN HABIT OF TWO SLEEPS

 1           For millennia, people slept in two shifts between nightfall and dawn. But why did this habit disappear?
 2           On 13 April 1699, in northern England, nine-year-old Jane Rowth and her mother woke up from their first sleep of the evening,
 3      just before two men came to their window. Mrs. Rowth whispered to Jane to stay quiet and promised to return in the morning, but
 4      she never came back; she was later found murdered. This story struck historian Roger Ekirch when he discovered Jane’s testimony
 5      in the judicial archives in the early 1990s, where she casually referenced her “first sleep.”
 6           Intrigued, Ekirch researched the phenomenon of biphasic sleep, finding that it was widely practiced throughout the preindustrial
 7      world. References to first and second sleeps appeared in various texts, including Chaucer’s The Canterbury Tales and works by
 8      notable figures such as Plutarch and Virgil. The habit was not limited to England; it appeared in cultures across Africa, South
 9      America, and Asia. The system of two sleeps was around for centuries, with the first mention dating back to the 8th century BC in
10      Homer’s Odyssey. Evidence of biphasic sleep can be found throughout history. For example, Christian monks adopted it for prayers
11      in the 6th century.
12           In the 17th century, a typical night involved going to bed from 9:00 to 11:00 pm, with people sleeping in shared spaces. Then,
13      they would naturally wake up for a period known as the watch, which lasted until about 1:00 am. The watch was used for various
14      activities, including household chores, socializing, and even sexual intimacy. This interval was also often a time for reflection,
15      prayer, or simply chatting with bed companions.
16           Ekirch’s research reached a crucial moment in 1995 when he read about a sleep experiment by Thomas Wehr. The study
17      involved men who, after weeks of limited light exposure, reverted to biphasic sleep, confirming Ekirch’s hypothesis about the
18      biological basis for this practice. More recent studies, including one in Madagascar, found that certain communities still follow
19      biphasic sleep patterns.
20           The shift away from this natural rhythm began with the Industrial Revolution, which introduced artificial lighting and altered
21      diurnal rhythms, leading to the consolidation of sleep into a single uninterrupted period. Although we may have lost the social and
22      philosophical aspects of biphasic sleep, modern conditions provide a different but arguably healthier sleeping experience.

 1           For millennia, people slept in two shifts between nightfall and dawn. But why did this habit disappear?
 2           On 13 April 1699, in northern England, nine-year-old Jane Rowth and her mother woke up from their first sleep of the evening,
 3      just before two men came to their window. Mrs. Rowth whispered to Jane to stay quiet and promised to return in the morning, but
 4      she never came back; she was later found murdered. This story struck historian Roger Ekirch when he discovered Jane’s testimony
 5      in the judicial archives in the early 1990s, where she casually referenced her “first sleep.”
 6           Intrigued, Ekirch researched the phenomenon of biphasic sleep, finding that it was widely practiced throughout the preindustrial
 7      world. References to first and second sleeps appeared in various texts, including Chaucer’s The Canterbury Tales and works by
 8      notable figures such as Plutarch and Virgil. The habit was not limited to England; it appeared in cultures across Africa, South
 9      America, and Asia. The system of two sleeps was around for centuries, with the first mention dating back to the 8th century BC in
10      Homer’s Odyssey. Evidence of biphasic sleep can be found throughout history. For example, Christian monks adopted it for prayers
11      in the 6th century.
12           In the 17th century, a typical night involved going to bed from 9:00 to 11:00 pm, with people sleeping in shared spaces. Then,
13      they would naturally wake up for a period known as the watch, which lasted until about 1:00 am. The watch was used for various
14      activities, including household chores, socializing, and even sexual intimacy. This interval was also often a time for reflection,
15      prayer, or simply chatting with bed companions.

18      biological basis for this practice. More recent studies, including one in Madagascar, found that certain communities still follow
19      biphasic sleep patterns.

21      diurnal rhythms, leading to the consolidation of sleep into a single uninterrupted period. Although we may have lost the social and

MONSIEUR TAN-TAN

 1          In 1840, Monsieur Leborgne arrived at the Bicêtre Hospital in Paris. He was a twenty-one-year-old man who exhibited an
 2      unusual linguistic problem: Whenever he was asked a question, he would always answer by saying one syllable twice, tan tan, in
 3      conjunction with quite varied intonation and expressive gestures. For this reason, the patient was nicknamed Tan-Tan. He spent
 4      the rest of his life hospitalized. Through the years, his condition deteriorated until, eventually, the limbs on his right side were
 5      paralyzed. On April 12, 1861, he was transferred to the surgery ward to be treated for gangrene. It was then that Pierre Paul Broca,
 6      a doctor who worked at the hospital, met him.
 7          One week earlier, Broca had participated in a meeting of the Anthropological Society. During this meeting, another doctor,
 8      Ernest Auburtin, presented some interesting studies about the possibility of identifying the location of language in the human brain.
 9      Auburtin was attempting to support the hypothesis that the brain did not work as a homogenous mass, at least when it came to
10      higher functions such as language. However, many scientists opposed the idea fiercely.
11          It did not take long for Broca to realize that the case he had come across constituted strong evidence to resolve the scientific
12      controversy. Although it was hard to determine exactly how much Tan-Tan understood, he clearly was able to interpret most of
13      what he heard. He could count and understand time. And neither his tongue nor his facial muscles had been affected by the
14      paralysis. In other words, Tan-Tan did not lack the cognitive or motor skills necessary for talking. His problem must have been,
15      therefore, a language-specific disability.
16          Tan-Tan died on April 17, 1861, and Broca did an autopsy of his brain. After careful examination, he concluded that a lesion
17      in Tan-Tan’s left frontal lobe must have been the cause of his loss of language. Broca had just discovered the first anatomic
18      evidence for the localization of a specific brain function. Shortly thereafter, he gave a talk at the Anthropological Society that would
19      change our conception of how the brain works. Although not all of Broca’s colleagues accepted his conclusions, the road to studying
20      the biological basis of language had already been taken. Modern neuroimaging techniques have shown that what is now known
21      as Broca’s area is critically involved in grammar processing, sentence construction, and the coordination of language-related
22      functions.
